content.problemStatement:
In the realm of emoticons, the Schrödinger smiley both smiles and frowns until its state is observed.

Given a string SSS consisting only of `:`, `(`, and `)` (colon, left parenthesis, and right parenthesis).

We define a Schrödinger's Smiley as any positive number of right parenthesis between two colons. For example, `:):`, `:))):`, and `:))))):` are Schrödinger's smileys while `:))(:`, `:(:`, `::):` and `::` are not.

Find the total number of substrings in SSS that are Schrödinger Smileys.

A substring is obtained by deleting any (possibly zero) number of characters from the beginning and any (possibly zero) number of characters from the end of the string.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, the length of the string SSS.
The next line contains the string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the total number of substrings in SSS that are Schrödinger Smileys.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 500000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the string S; the second line contains the string S consisting only of characters ':', '(' and ')'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the total number of substrings of S that are Schrödinger Smileys.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: No ')' between any two colons, answer 0. Test case 2: The substring S[3..7] = :))): matches the pattern, answer 1. Test case 3: Two substrings match: S[1..4] = :)): and S[4..6] = :):, answer 2.

solution.approach: A Schrödinger Smiley is exactly the pattern ':'+k*')'+':' with k >= 1 and no other characters in between. While scanning the string we look for a ':' at position i, then count the consecutive ')' characters that follow it. If at least one ')' is found and the character immediately after this run is another ':', we have found one valid substring. Each such occurrence is independent, so we simply increment the answer and continue scanning from the next position. This yields O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. Overall O(total N) for all test cases.
